dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 8422 IH: Clean Legislating and Ethical Accountability Now Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mr. Pappas (for himself and Mr. Harrigan) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on House Administration, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned

form.official-title: To enact House Resolution 895, One Hundred Tenth Congress, (establishing the Office of Congressional Ethics) into permanent law.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Clean Legislating and Ethical Accountability Now Act or the CLEAN Act.

section.header: Enacting House Resolution 895 into permanent law
section.text: House Resolution 895, One Hundred Tenth Congress, is hereby enacted into permanent law, except that—

paragraph.text: the Office of Congressional Ethics shall be treated as a standing committee of the House of Representatives for purposes of section 202(i) of the Legislative Reorganization Act of 1946 (2 U.S.C. 4301(i));
references to the Committee on Standards of Official Conduct shall be construed as references to the Committee on Ethics;
no person shall serve as a member of the board for more than 4 2-year terms;
a vacancy on the board shall be filled not later than 60 calendar days after the date any such vacancy occurs, including any vacancy that is the result of term limits;
if a vacancy on the board is not filled pursuant to the deadline under paragraph (4), the board shall not be prohibited from operating unless there are fewer than 3 members on the board;
any member of the board currently serving a term in excess of the limitations under paragraph (3) shall be removed from the board not later than 30 days after the date of the enactment of this Act;

any individual who is the subject of a preliminary review or second-phase review by the board shall be informed of the right to be represented by counsel and invoking that right should not be held negatively against them; and
the Office may not take any action that would deny any person any right or protection provided under the Constitution of the United States.
